subject: 2Africa Submarine Cable
executive_summary:
2Africa is the world's longest submarine cable at 45,000 kilometers, connecting 46 landing stations across 33 countries in Africa, Asia, and Europe. Led by Meta and backed by a consortium including Bayobab (MTN Group), center3 (stc Group), China Mobile International, Orange, Telecom Egypt, Vodafone, and WIOCC, the system was announced in 2020 and its core infrastructure was completed in November 2025 after a six-year engineering mission. The cable employs spatial division multiplexing (SDM) technology with up to 16 fiber pairs per cable — double the capacity of older systems — delivering up to 180 Tbps of capacity on key segments.

2Africa is the first submarine cable to connect East and West Africa in a continuous system while also linking the continent to the Middle East, South Asia, and Europe. The project is expected to contribute up to USD 36.9 billion to Africa's GDP within two to three years of full operation, connecting over 3 billion people. The 2Africa Pearls extension — reaching into the Gulf states, Pakistan, and India — is scheduled to go live in 2026, though Red Sea segments have been delayed by Houthi militant activity that has made cable-laying operations unsafe in the Bab al-Mandab Strait.

The cable represents a transformative but geopolitically complex investment. Meta's dominance of the project raises concerns about digital colonialism and the concentration of African internet infrastructure under a single US technology company. Simultaneously, China Mobile International's consortium membership introduces US-China tension dynamics. The Red Sea delays — which have prevented landings in Sudan and western Saudi Arabia — demonstrate how regional conflicts can disrupt even the most ambitious global infrastructure projects, with the estimated project cost exceeding $2 billion.

key_findings.headline: World's Largest Cable Project Reaches Core Completion Despite Geopolitical Obstacles
key_findings.detail: In November 2025, Meta announced the completion of 2Africa's core infrastructure — the main loop around Africa connecting to Europe. This represents one of the largest private infrastructure investments in African connectivity history. The system deploys SDM technology supporting 16 fiber pairs per cable (double legacy systems), with the West segment (England to South Africa) delivering 168 Tbps across 8 fiber pairs at 21 Tbps per pair, and Mediterranean segments reaching 180+ Tbps across 16 pairs at 30+ Tbps per pair. The cable spans 50 jurisdictions and reaches over 3 billion people.

key_findings.headline: Red Sea Conflict Blocks Critical Pearls Extension and Regional Landings
key_findings.detail: Houthi militant activity in the Red Sea has made it impossible to safely deploy cable-laying ships through the Bab al-Mandab Strait, halting work on the 2Africa Pearls extension and preventing planned landings in Sudan and western Saudi Arabia. The Pearls segment — designed to connect the African loop through the Gulf states, Pakistan, and India — is indefinitely delayed in the Red Sea region, though it is scheduled for completion in 2026. The delays represent a significant challenge for an estimated $2+ billion project and reduce the connectivity benefits for Gulf and South Asian markets.

key_findings.headline: Meta's Dominant Role Raises Digital Sovereignty Concerns Across Africa
key_findings.detail: Meta is the primary investor and project leader of 2Africa, making it one of the largest private investors in undersea cable infrastructure globally. Critics argue that Meta's interest in connecting African markets is inseparable from its commercial interest in capturing African users for its social media platforms. With approximately 60% of Sub-Saharan Africa's population (over 700 million people) within mobile broadband range but not online, the cable creates the physical infrastructure for massive platform expansion. This raises digital sovereignty concerns about African internet infrastructure being controlled by a foreign technology company.

key_findings.headline: China Mobile International Membership Introduces US-China Tension Dynamics
key_findings.detail: China Mobile International is a founding consortium member of 2Africa, creating tension with US-led efforts to exclude Chinese companies from critical communications infrastructure. While China Mobile and China Telecom were forced out of the SEA-ME-WE 6 project under US pressure, CMI has maintained its position in 2Africa. This dual dynamic — a US tech giant (Meta) partnering with a Chinese state-owned telecom — illustrates the pragmatic compromises required for pan-continental cable projects that cross multiple geopolitical spheres of influence.

key_findings.headline: Egypt Emerges as Non-Negotiable Geopolitical Chokepoint
key_findings.detail: Over 90% of all data traffic between Europe and Asia transits through Egyptian territory, accounting for approximately 17% of total global internet traffic. 2Africa relies heavily on Egyptian landing points and terrestrial crossings, with Telecom Egypt as a key consortium member. This concentration creates a single-country dependency that could be exploited for political leverage, surveillance, or disrupted by domestic instability. Egypt's position as the bridge between the Red Sea and Mediterranean makes it the most critical chokepoint in the entire 2Africa system.

key_findings.headline: Open-Access Model Could Transform African Digital Economics
key_findings.detail: The 2Africa consortium operates on an open-access model, ensuring multiple service providers can purchase capacity on the cable. This approach is designed to foster competition, reduce prices, and prevent the monopolistic practices that have historically characterized African telecommunications. The projected USD 36.9 billion GDP contribution within 2-3 years of operation and the 50% deeper cable burial (for physical protection) demonstrate both the economic ambition and engineering investment in the project's long-term success.

recent_developments.headline: Meta Announces Completion of Core 2Africa System
recent_developments.summary: Meta announced the completion of the core 2Africa submarine cable system after nearly six years of engineering and deployment across 50 jurisdictions. The core system — the main loop around Africa connecting to Europe — is now operational with 46 landing stations in 33 countries. The 2Africa Pearls extension into the Gulf, Pakistan, and India is scheduled to go live in 2026, bringing the total system length to 45,000 km. The announcement highlighted SDM technology enabling 16 fiber pairs per cable with up to 180 Tbps capacity.
recent_developments.impact: Marks the activation of the world's largest submarine cable system, providing transformative bandwidth increases for 33 countries across three continents.

recent_developments.headline: Red Sea Conflict Forces Delay of 2Africa Pearls Extension
recent_developments.summary: Houthi militant activity in the Red Sea forced the postponement of cable-laying operations for the 2Africa Pearls extension segment that would transit the Bab al-Mandab Strait. Cable-laying ships cannot operate safely in the conflict zone, preventing planned landings in Sudan and western Saudi Arabia. The delays affect the Pearls segment connecting the Gulf states, Pakistan, and India to the African loop. The Iran-Israel conflict further compounded risks in the broader Middle East maritime environment.
recent_developments.impact: Indefinite delay of critical Gulf and South Asian connectivity, reducing the economic and strategic value of the 2Africa system for key markets until Red Sea operations become safe.

recent_developments.headline: East African Submarine Cable Outages Highlight Continental Vulnerability
recent_developments.summary: In March 2024, ten African countries lost internet connectivity due to disruptions in four undersea cables (WACS, ACE, MainOne, SAT3) in West and Southern Africa. While 2Africa was not directly affected, the outages underscored the critical importance of the 2Africa system as a redundant, high-capacity alternative for African connectivity. The incident demonstrated that Africa's internet infrastructure remains dangerously concentrated on a small number of aging cables, validating the strategic rationale for 2Africa's investment.
recent_developments.impact: Validated 2Africa's strategic importance and accelerated demand for its capacity as African nations recognized their vulnerability to cable disruptions.

risk_assessment.current_risk: High. While the core system is operational, the critical Pearls extension remains blocked by Red Sea conflict, leaving Gulf and South Asian connectivity incomplete. The cable's strategic dependence on Egyptian transit, its exposure to Red Sea chokepoint risks, and the geopolitical complexity of a Meta-led consortium including China Mobile International all contribute to an elevated risk profile. The project's $2+ billion investment and the connectivity needs of 3 billion people create enormous stakes.
key_drivers: Red Sea conflict preventing completion of Pearls extension and landings in Sudan/western Saudi Arabia
Egyptian chokepoint dependency for 90%+ of Europe-Asia data transit
Meta's dominant role raising digital sovereignty concerns among African governments
China Mobile International membership creating US-China geopolitical tension within the consortium
Affordability barriers preventing 700+ million Sub-Saharan Africans from accessing the connectivity 2Africa provides
mitigation_actions: Develop alternative routing for Pearls extension that bypasses the Red Sea chokepoint (e.g., overland through Saudi Arabia)
Diversify Egyptian transit points and invest in terrestrial bypass routes
Strengthen open-access governance to prevent any single consortium member from controlling capacity allocation
Support African regulatory frameworks that ensure digital sovereignty over data transiting through 2Africa
Invest in last-mile infrastructure and affordability programs to maximize the cable's economic impact
Maintain naval coordination with regional powers to protect cable-laying operations when Red Sea conditions permit
